APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—discourse-aware text categorization—by integrating RST discourse trees with a recursive neural architecture and attention, which is squarely within top conference scope. The methodology, datasets (including a large 650k corpus, movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, and legislative bills), baselines (HAN, prior discourse-motivated and structured methods), and evaluation (accuracy with cross-validation) match expectations for a top venue. While it depends on automatically produced discourse trees (DPLP trained on 347 WSJ articles) and shows sensitivity to reduced RST training data, these are limitations of data/coverage rather than venue-fit, so overall it is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference. | B(5): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic—discourse-aware text categorization—using contemporary machine learning methods (recursive neural networks with attention) and compares against strong baselines (Hierarchical Attention Networks, prior discourse models). It evaluates on both large-scale (650k) and standard benchmarks (movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, legislative bills) with appropriate metrics (accuracy, cross-validation) and reports hyperparameters, indicating methods and evaluation meet venue standards. The work is positioned within relevant prior work (Yogatama & Smith, Yessenalina et al., Card et al.) and discusses important limitations (dependence on DPLP trained on WSJ), so it is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

While it depends on automatically produced discourse trees (DPLP trained on 347 WSJ articles) and shows sensitivity to reduced RST training data, these are limitations of data/coverage rather than venue-fit, so overall it is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

The work is positioned within relevant prior work (Yogatama & Smith, Yessenalina et al., Card et al.) and discusses important limitations (dependence on DPLP trained on WSJ), so it is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are broad and compare against many baselines across multiple datasets (including a very large 650k set, movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, and legislative bills), which supports the main claims. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks are present (an UNLABELED variant and an analysis showing degradation if the RST treebank is reduced to 25%), although more fine-grained ablations of composition choices could be missing. Related work is described as thorough, and limitations are explicitly discussed (dependence on the DPLP parser and its small WSJ training set). Reproducibility is facilitated by reported hyperparameters, but code is not mentioned, so full replication may require extra effort. | B(4): The experiments are fairly comprehensive: the model is evaluated across multiple, diverse datasets (large preprocessed corpus, movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, legislative bills) and compared to strong baselines (HAN, prior discourse and regularization methods). Appropriate ablations/sensitivity analyses are included (an UNLABELED variant, comparison to flat models, and a reported sensitivity to amount of RST training data showing degradation when the RST treebank is reduced). Related work is described thoroughly and limitations (dependence on DPLP and its WSJ training domain) are discussed, but reproducibility is slightly weakened because code is not mentioned even though hyperparameters are reported.

Reproducibility is facilitated by reported hyperparameters, but code is not mentioned, so full replication may require extra effort.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and follows a clear flow from problem and main claims to method and experiments (the summary lists precise methodological steps and a comprehensive experimental setup). The main contributions are stated early and the notation/method description come across as consistent and well-defined (e.g., EDU encoding, discourse-to-dependency conversion, relation-specific composition matrices, and attention mechanism are all clearly described). Figures/tables and datasets/baselines seem to be used effectively to present results, and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related work coverage, so only minor presentation polishing would be needed. | B(4): The summary indicates a clear, logical organization (intro, detailed methodology, comprehensive experiments, conclusion) and that the main contributions are stated early (discourse-RST benefit, recursive network with attention, unlabeled variant). Notation and method descriptions appear consistent and well-defined (EDU vectors, relation-specific matrices, attention weights) and supporting materials—datasets, baselines, metrics, and hyperparameters—are reported, suggesting effective figures/tables and examples. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough, so only minor presentation density in technical sections prevents an exceptional rating.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable novelty by combining RST-style discourse dependency trees produced by an existing parser with a recursive neural network and a learned, non-normalized attention mechanism that operationalizes prior hand-crafted weighting schemes. The unlabeled model variant and the end-to-end learned, non-competitive attention are clear differentiators from prior discourse-motivated and hierarchical attention approaches (e.g., Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al., Yang et al.). While text categorization itself is well-studied and the method depends on an external discourse parser (limiting originality in data dependence), the specific architectural choices represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would inform experts in the field. | B(4): The paper brings a distinct method by composing RST-derived discourse dependency trees with relation-specific recursive neural composition and a learned, non-normalized attention mechanism — a contrast to prior work that relied on hand-crafted weighting or flat/hierarchical attention (e.g., Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al., Yang et al.). The inclusion of an UNLABELED variant that omits relation labels to reduce parameters is a concrete, novel modeling choice balancing inductive bias and complexity. These contributions clearly differentiate the work from cited baselines and represent notable novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of existing techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a notable novelty by combining RST-style discourse dependency trees produced by an existing parser with a recursive neural network and a learned, non-normalized attention mechanism that operationalizes prior hand-crafted weighting schemes. The unlabeled model variant and the end-to-end learned, non-competitive attention are clear differentiators from prior discourse-motivated and hierarchical attention approaches (e.g., Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al., Yang et al.). While text categorization itself is well-studied and the method depends on an external discourse parser (limiting originality in data dependence), the specific architectural choices represent a meaningful, non-trivial extension that would inform experts in the field.

The inclusion of an UNLABELED variant that omits relation labels to reduce parameters is a concrete, novel modeling choice balancing inductive bias and complexity. These contributions clearly differentiate the work from cited baselines and represent notable novelty rather than a purely incremental combination of existing techniques.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper addresses an important problem—incorporating RST-style discourse into text categorization—by proposing a recursive neural model over DPLP-produced discourse trees with a learned non-normalized attention mechanism and an unlabeled variant that automates prior hand-crafted weighting. The approach is evaluated on diverse datasets including a large 650k corpus, movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, and legislative bills, indicating practical relevance and potential to shift how researchers weight discourse units. However, its reliance on automatically produced discourse trees (DPLP trained on only 347 WSJ articles) and demonstrated sensitivity to the amount of RST training data (performance drops if the RST treebank is reduced to 25%) constrain immediate broad adoption. Overall this suggests moderate impact: a useful, promising direction that opens follow-up work but is not immediately transformative. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—how to incorporate discourse (RST) structure into neural text-classification—and provides strong empirical evidence across multiple datasets (including a large 650k example set and several smaller corpora) that parser-produced RST trees and a recursive attention-based model improve categorization versus flat and hand-crafted baselines. The proposed unlabeled variant and non-normalized attention automate prior weighting schemes and offer a practical inductive bias that other researchers can build on. However, the approach’s dependence on automatically produced discourse trees (DPLP trained on only 347 WSJ articles) and sensitivity to reduced RST-treebank size (performance falls below discourse-ignorant models at 25% size) constrain its generality and immediate widescale adoption.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—incorporating RST-style discourse into text categorization—by proposing a recursive neural model over DPLP-produced discourse trees with a learned non-normalized attention mechanism and an unlabeled variant that automates prior hand-crafted weighting. The approach is evaluated on diverse datasets including a large 650k corpus, movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, and legislative bills, indicating practical relevance and potential to shift how researchers weight discourse units. However, its reliance on automatically produced discourse trees (DPLP trained on only 347 WSJ articles) and demonstrated sensitivity to the amount of RST training data (performance drops if the RST treebank is reduced to 25%) constrain immediate broad adoption. Overall this suggests moderate impact: a useful, promising direction that opens follow-up work but is not immediately transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—how to incorporate discourse (RST) structure into neural text-classification—and provides strong empirical evidence across multiple datasets (including a large 650k example set and several smaller corpora) that parser-produced RST trees and a recursive attention-based model improve categorization versus flat and hand-crafted baselines. The proposed unlabeled variant and non-normalized attention automate prior weighting schemes and offer a practical inductive bias that other researchers can build on. However, the approach’s dependence on automatically produced discourse trees (DPLP trained on only 347 WSJ articles) and sensitivity to reduced RST-treebank size (performance falls below discourse-ignorant models at 25% size) constrain its generality and immediate widescale adoption.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: experiments across multiple datasets (including a large 650k dataset, MR, MFC, debates, and legislative bills) compare against appropriate baselines (e.g. Yang et al. 2016 and prior discourse models) and report accuracy measures. The technical method (BiLSTM EDU encodings, DPLP-produced discourse dependency trees, recursive composition with relation-specific matrices and non-normalized attention) is coherent and correctly specified; there are no apparent mathematical errors in the described model. Minor methodological concerns remain: the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, and the approach depends strongly on an automatically produced parser (DPLP trained on only 347 WSJ articles) — the authors themselves note sensitivity to reduced RST training data. These issues do not overturn the main experimental findings, hence a slightly reduced score. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental evidence across multiple datasets and competitive baselines (e.g., Yang et al., Bhatia et al., Yessenalina et al.), and the methodology (EDU BiLSTM encoding, DPLP-produced RST trees, relation-specific composition and attention) is coherent and plausibly implemented. The mathematical model is straightforward (composition matrices and learned attention) so no evident derivation errors are indicated in the summary; however, no complex proofs were required or reported. The experimental methodology is generally sound (diverse datasets, proper baselines, 10-fold CV reported), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the approach is clearly sensitive to the discourse parser and RST training data (DPLP was trained on only 347 WSJ documents and the model underperforms if the RST treebank is reduced to 25%), which are notable limitations. Overall the conclusions are justified by the results but tempered by the parser/domain dependence and the missing details on statistical significance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and empirically supported: experiments across multiple datasets (including a large 650k dataset, MR, MFC, debates, and legislative bills) compare against appropriate baselines (e.g. Yang et al. 2016 and prior discourse models) and report accuracy measures. The technical method (BiLSTM EDU encodings, DPLP-produced discourse dependency trees, recursive composition with relation-specific matrices and non-normalized attention) is coherent and correctly specified; there are no apparent mathematical errors in the described model. Minor methodological concerns remain: the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, and the approach depends strongly on an automatically produced parser (DPLP trained on only 347 WSJ articles) — the authors themselves note sensitivity to reduced RST training data. These issues do not overturn the main experimental findings, hence a slightly reduced score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experimental evidence across multiple datasets and competitive baselines (e.g., Yang et al., Bhatia et al., Yessenalina et al.), and the methodology (EDU BiLSTM encoding, DPLP-produced RST trees, relation-specific composition and attention) is coherent and plausibly implemented. The mathematical model is straightforward (composition matrices and learned attention) so no evident derivation errors are indicated in the summary; however, no complex proofs were required or reported. The experimental methodology is generally sound (diverse datasets, proper baselines, 10-fold CV reported), but the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the approach is clearly sensitive to the discourse parser and RST training data (DPLP was trained on only 347 WSJ documents and the model underperforms if the RST treebank is reduced to 25%), which are notable limitations. Overall the conclusions are justified by the results but tempered by the parser/domain dependence and the missing details on statistical significance.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares to a comprehensive set of relevant and recent baselines (e.g., hierarchical attention networks of Yang et al. 2016, Bhatia et al. 2015, Hogenboom et al. 2015, Yogatama & Smith 2014, Card et al. 2016, Yessenalina et al. 2010). Comparisons use multiple standard datasets (Zhang et al. 2015 preprocessing, Pang & Lee movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, legislative bills) and the same evaluation metrics (accuracy, average accuracy), with established splits noted (e.g., Yessenalina split), supporting fairness. The paper acknowledges when prior methods can perform comparably or better (e.g., dependence on DPLP and underperformance when RST treebank is reduced to 25%) and analyzes differences (UNLABELED variant, relation-specific composition, and contrast to hand-crafted weighting), and its related-work coverage is described as thorough. | B(5): The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (e.g., Hierarchical Attention Networks / Yang et al. 2016, Bhatia et al. 2015, Hogenboom et al. 2015, Yogatama & Smith 2014, Card et al. 2016, Yessenalina et al. 2010) across multiple datasets (movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, legislative bills, large preprocessed set) using the same accuracy metrics/10-fold CV. It explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods outperform it (e.g., when the RST treebank is reduced to 25% the proposed method underperforms Yang et al. 2016) and analyzes design differences (labelled vs unlabeled variants, attention normalization, relation-specific composition) relative to hand-crafted discourse weighting approaches. Related work coverage is described as thorough, indicating relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed.

Comparisons use multiple standard datasets (Zhang et al. 2015 preprocessing, Pang & Lee movie reviews, MFC, congressional debates, legislative bills) and the same evaluation metrics (accuracy, average accuracy), with established splits noted (e.g., Yessenalina split), supporting fairness. The paper acknowledges when prior methods can perform comparably or better (e.g., dependence on DPLP and underperformance when RST treebank is reduced to 25%) and analyzes differences (UNLABELED variant, relation-specific composition, and contrast to hand-crafted weighting), and its related-work coverage is described as thorough.

It explicitly acknowledges cases where prior methods outperform it (e.g., when the RST treebank is reduced to 25% the proposed method underperforms Yang et al. 2016) and analyzes design differences (labelled vs unlabeled variants, attention normalization, relation-specific composition) relative to hand-crafted discourse weighting approaches. Related work coverage is described as thorough, indicating relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed.

main_claims: Discourse structure as defined by Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST) and produced by an existing discourse parser (DPLP) benefits text categorization.
A recursive neural network built on a discourse dependency tree with an attention mechanism can compute document representations that focus on salient EDUs for categorization.
An UNLABELED model variant that omits relation labels reduces parameters and still provides a discourse-based inductive bias between fully labeled discourse models and flat document structures.

The proposed attention mechanism does not normalize weights across siblings (reflecting RST’s lack of competition among satellites) and is learned end-to-end, automating prior hand-crafted weighting schemes.
_summary.methodology: Encode each EDU with a bidirectional LSTM to get EDU vectors, transform RST trees into discourse dependency trees via DPLP, and recursively compose EDU and child subtree vectors using relation-specific composition matrices and a learned (non-normalized) attention weight; classify the root representation with a softmax.
datasets: Preprocessed dataset from Zhang et al. (2015) with 650,000 training and 50,000 test examples
Movie review corpus (Pang and Lee, 2004) — 1,000 positive and 1,000 negative reviews
Media Frames Corpus (MFC; Card et al., 2015) — ~4,200 news articles about immigration
Congressional floor debates corpus (Thomas et al., 2006; data split by Yessenalina et al., 2010)
Legislative bills corpus (Yano et al., 2012) — 51,762 bills (103rd to 111th US Congresses)
baselines: Bhatia et al. (2015) (discourse-motivated models with hand-crafted weighting)
Hogenboom et al. (2015) (manually-designed weighting schemes and lexicon-based model)
limitations: Method depends on automatically produced discourse trees from a parser (DPLP); performance relies on the quality and training data of that parser.
DPLP as distributed is trained on 347 Wall Street Journal articles from the Penn Treebank, which constrains the parser's domain and coverage.

If the RST discourse treebank were reduced to 25% of its size, the proposed method would underperform a discourse-ignorant model (Yang et al., 2016), indicating sensitivity to amount of discourse-annotated training data.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - discourse-aware text categorization (including sentiment analysis)
